desc: Iridescent blue-black swallowtail. Stray/rare colonist in SoCal — no native Aristolochia. Toxic; model for a Batesian mimicry complex. Established only where pipevine is cultivated.

fm.Upperside: Iridescent blue-black; single row of pale hindwing spots
fm.Underside: Vivid orange submarginal spots on iridescent blue hindwing

hp: Larvae on Aristolochia californica (pipevine). Sequesters toxins for chemical defense. Mimicry specialist.

desc: Adaptable swallowtail equally at home hilltop-patrolling rocky chaparral summits and visiting suburban fennel gardens. Yellow-and-black with blue hindwing spots and red-orange eyespot. Larvae on Apiaceae — native lomatium and introduced fennel. Common year-round.

fm.Upperside: Black with broad yellow band; blue/orange hindwing eyespot
fm.Underside: Yellow spots may be cream or orange-tinged

hp: Larvae on Apiaceae — fennel, parsley, citrus, Lomatium. Black swallowtail relative. Aposematic caterpillar with osmeterium defense.

desc: Dark, short-tailed swallowtail of arid rocky habitats. LA County populations restricted to upper desert canyons.

fm.Upperside: Black with reduced cream bands; short stubby tails
fm.Underside: Blue and orange hindwing spots on dark ground
fm.Habitat: Rocky canyon walls, arid hillsides above 3,500'

hp: Apiaceae — Lomatium, Pteryxia. Important prey for insectivorous birds.

desc: Iconic large yellow swallowtail with bold black tiger stripes. Graceful sailing flight along riparian corridors. Larvae on sycamore, willow, cottonwood, and alder. Common along any LA County stream with mature trees. Spring through fall.

fm.Upperside: Bright yellow with bold black tiger stripes; single tail
fm.Underside: Blue and orange marginal hindwing spots

hp: Platanus, salix, populus. Riparian. Important prey for insectivorous birds.

desc: Cream-white swallowtail replacing the Tiger Swallowtail at higher elevations in the San Gabriel Mountains. Broad black stripes on pale cream (not yellow) ground. On ceanothus and other chaparral shrubs. Strong sailing flight along mountain ridges.

fm.Upperside: Cream-white with black tiger stripes; blue/orange marginal spots
fm.Underside: Cream with subdued markings

hp: Larvae on Ceanothus (California lilac). Native chaparral specialist. In chaparral habitat. Pale.

desc: The largest western swallowtail, with two tails per hindwing (diagnostic — all other western swallowtails have one). Wingspan to 5.5 inches. Rare stray to LA County from desert mountain ranges. On ash and chokecherry.

fm.Upperside: Yellow with narrow stripes; 2 tails per hindwing
fm.Underside: Yellow with blue marginal crescents
fm.Habitat: Desert riparian; vagrant to coastal LA

hp: Fraxinus, Prunus, Ligustrum. Important prey for insectivorous birds.

desc: Introduced European butterfly, now the most abundant butterfly in urban LA. Found in every garden, park, and vacant lot. Larvae eat brassicas.

fm.Upperside: White with black forewing tips; 1 spot (♂) or 2 (♀)
fm.Underside: Pale greenish-yellow hindwing

hp: Larvae on Brassicaceae — cabbage, broccoli, kale, nasturtium. Introduced from Europe. Most common garden butterfly pest.

desc: Boldly checkered white of weedy disturbed habitats. More heavily marked than Cabbage White. On mustards. Declining in some areas.

fm.Upperside: White with heavy charcoal checkering; ♀ darker
fm.Underside: Olive-green vein markings on hindwing

hp: Brassicaceae: mustards, Sisymbrium spp.

desc: Delicate, lightly marked white butterfly of montane chaparral. Distinguished from Checkered White by much lighter markings — only faint gray-green marbling below. Single-brooded spring flier (March-May) on mustard family plants. San Gabriel Mountains.

fm.Upperside: White with light gray-green vein-end markings
fm.Underside: Hindwing veins yellow-green outlined

hp: Brassicaceae — Descurainia, Arabis

desc: Spring ephemeral with vivid orange wingtips that signal wildflower season has arrived. Males patrol hillsides seeking females. On mustards.

fm.Upperside: White with vivid orange forewing tips (♂); reduced (♀)
fm.Underside: Hindwing marbled green-brown (cryptic)

hp: Brassicaceae — Sisymbrium, Arabis

desc: Small, subtly marbled white butterfly. Distinguished from Sara Orangetip by complete lack of orange wing tips. Hindwing underside has gray-green marbling in irregular bands. On Brassicaceae in chaparral. Single spring brood. San Gabriel foothills.

fm.Upperside: White; gray-tipped forewing apex (no orange)
fm.Underside: Finely marbled gray-green; no orange

hp: Brassicaceae — Arabis, Streptanthus

desc: Abundant golden-orange butterfly of every alfalfa field, park lawn, and weedy lot in LA. Breeds continuously year-round. Often confused with Cloudless Sulphur.

fm.Upperside: Orange with wide black borders; pink fringes
fm.Underside: Yellow-green with silver cell spot ringed in pink

hp: Fabaceae: alfalfa (Medicago sativa), clovers (Trifolium), vetches

desc: The California state butterfly. Male's forewing pattern distinctly resembles a poodle's face in silhouette — dark border with yellow center and pink-purple iridescence. Female plain yellow. On false indigo (Amorpha californica). Foothill chaparral.

fm.Upperside: ♂: yellow with purple-black "dogface" pattern. ♀: plain yellow.
fm.Underside: Yellow to greenish; small pink cell spot

hp: Part of local invertebrate food web. Associated with False Indigo.

desc: Smallest North American pierid. Dainty low flight through weedy lots and desert flats. Yellow with dark forewing tips. Year-round in warm areas.

fm.Upperside: Yellow with black forewing tips
fm.Underside: Winter form darker, heavily dusted

hp: Dainty sulphur — smallest NA pierid. On Asteraceae (Bidens, Dyssodia, Tagetes). Low darting flight through weedy lots and desert flats.

desc: Large bright yellow neotropical butterfly. Colonizes SoCal in warm years, sometimes in spectacular northward migrations. On cassia and senna.

fm.Upperside: ♂: unmarked bright yellow. ♀: paler.
fm.Underside: Yellow-green; reddish cell spot

hp: Fabaceae: Senna spp., Cassia spp.

desc: Small copper butterfly with purple iridescence on males in fresh condition. Wetland edges and moist meadows. Caterpillars on dock and knotweed.

fm.Upperside: ♂: dark with purple sheen/orange band. ♀: orange, heavy spotting.
fm.Underside: Tan with black spots; orange submarginal band

hp: Rumex, Polygonum (docks, knotweeds)

desc: The most widespread hairstreak in North America. Plain gray underside with fine black-and-white postmedian line and orange-capped hindwing eyespot. Larvae bore into flower buds of an enormous range of plants (>50 families). Year-round in LA. Tails short. Quick, erratic flight.

fm.Upperside: Blue-gray (♂); darker gray (♀)
fm.Underside: Pale gray with white-edged postmedian line; orange spots

hp: Gray hairstreak — most widespread NA hairstreak. Polyphagous larvae on Fabaceae, Malvaceae, Rosaceae flower buds. Ant-tended.

desc: Large brilliant iridescent blue hairstreak. Perches high in oak canopy near clumps of mistletoe (larval host). Rarely descends. Stunning when seen.

fm.Upperside: Brilliant iridescent blue; red basal spots
fm.Underside: Black with red-orange basal spots; blue-green highlights

hp: Larvae on Quercus agrifolia (coast live oak). Oak woodland habitat. On oaks (Quercus).

desc: Warm brown hairstreak with orange hindwing crescents. Associated with ceanothus, oak, and mountain mahogany. Males hilltop aggressively.

fm.Upperside: Brown with faint orange marginal spots
fm.Underside: Brown-gray; black spot row edged white; orange crescents

hp: Larvae on Ceanothus (California lilac). Native chaparral specialist. Associated with Oak.

desc: Golden-brown hairstreak strongly associated with ceanothus (California lilac). Males hilltop. Spring flier in chaparral and woodland edges.

fm.Upperside: Warm golden-brown
fm.Underside: Golden-brown with postmedian line

hp: Larvae on Ceanothus (California lilac). Native chaparral specialist. In chaparral habitat. Hedgerow.

desc: Tiny hairstreak with dazzling emerald green underside when fresh. Fades to gray-brown. On buckwheat and lotus in chaparral. Spring flier.

fm.Upperside: Brown (rarely seen)
fm.Underside: Bright emerald green fading to olive; white line

hp: Lotus scoparius, Eriogonum fasciculatum

desc: Western pygmy-blue — the smallest butterfly in North America with a wingspan barely reaching ½ inch. Pale brown with blue basal scaling. On saltbush and pigweed in weedy lots.

fm.Upperside: Copper-brown with blue basal wash
fm.Underside: Brown base, white outer; 3 hindwing eyespots
fm.Habitat: Salt marshes, alkali flats, weedy lots

hp: Chenopodiaceae — Suaeda, Atriplex

desc: Tiny iridescent blue butterfly in constant low flight through urban gardens. Extremely common. Larvae on leadwort and legumes. Year-round in warm areas.

fm.Upperside: Lavender-blue (♂); brown with blue basal (♀)
fm.Underside: Pale with brown/white banding; 2 eyespots
fm.Habitat: Urban gardens, weedy lots, coastal scrub

hp: Plumbago, Medicago, Astragalus

desc: Spring-flying pale blue of riparian corridors and chaparral edges. Among the first blues each year. On ceanothus, buckeye, and other shrubs.

fm.Upperside: Pale silvery-blue with narrow dark margin
fm.Underside: Pale gray with faint dark spots; no orange

hp: Larvae on Ceanothus (California lilac). Native chaparral specialist. In chaparral habitat. Echo.

desc: Spring-flying blue of open woodland with prominent round black spots on pale blue-gray underside. On lupines and other legumes. Single early-spring brood.

fm.Upperside: Silvery-blue (♂); brown with blue basal (♀)
fm.Underside: Gray with single row of prominent round black spots

hp: Lupinus, Astragalus, Lathyrus, Vicia

desc: Buckwheat specialist. Distinguished by squarish metallic marginal spots on hindwing underside. Multiple subspecies across SoCal.

fm.Upperside: Blue (♂) or brown (♀)
fm.Underside: Orange band with squarish metallic spots
fm.Flight Period: May–Aug; single brood, sync with buckwheat

hp: Eriogonum flowers (oligolectic)

desc: Western migratory Monarch — IUCN Endangered, USFWS candidate. LA County hosts coastal winter roosts. Population collapsed >95%.

fm.Upperside: Deep orange with black veins; white spots in margins
fm.Underside: Paler; hindwing golden-brown

hp: Larvae on Asclepias (milkweed). Western monarch overwintering population declined 99.9% by 2020 (Xerces Society). Prey for orioles, grosbeaks.

desc: The monarch's smaller, darker relative. Desert-edge milkweed-feeder. Rich mahogany brown. Males have black scent patch on hindwing. Less common than Monarch.

fm.Upperside: Rich chestnut-brown; white forewing spots; no black veining
fm.Underside: Chestnut with veins outlined in black

hp: Larvae on Asclepias (milkweed) and Funastrum (milkvine). Sequesters cardiac glycosides. Non-migratory resident in SoCal.

desc: Brilliant orange butterfly with extraordinary silvered underside — elongated metallic silver spots unlike any other NA butterfly. Not a true fritillary (Heliconiinae, not Argynninae). Abundant wherever Passiflora grows. Year-round in LA. Caterpillars orange-and-black, spiny.

fm.Upperside: Bright orange; 3 white-centered black forewing spots
fm.Underside: Large metallic silver spots (spectacular)

hp: Passifloraceae: Passiflora spp. (passionflower vines)

desc: Flat-winged tawny fritillary with low sailing flight. Unlike true fritillaries (Argynnis), lacks metallic silver spots on hindwing underside.

fm.Upperside: Tawny with dark zigzag lines; no silver
fm.Underside: Mottled brown/cream; dark median band

hp: Passifloraceae, Violaceae, Plantaginaceae

desc: World's most widespread butterfly. Spectacular northward irruptions — 2019 triggered billions of migrants through LA.

fm.Upperside: Orange-brown; white forewing patches; irregular black tips
fm.Underside: Complex mosaic; 4–5 small hindwing eyespots

hp: Larvae on thistles (Cirsium), Malva, Urtica. Mass migration events. One of world's most widespread butterflies. Nectar on many flowers.

desc: LA's resident lady butterfly (unlike the migratory Painted Lady). Isolated orange costal bar on forewing diagnostic. On mallows. Year-round.

fm.Upperside: Orange; isolated orange costal bar (diagnostic)
fm.Underside: Complex pattern; small eyespots

hp: Malvaceae: mallows (Malva, Sphaeralcea); Urticaceae: nettle

desc: Unmistakable black butterfly with vivid red-orange forewing band forming a diagonal slash, and white forewing spots. Males aggressively territorial — defend perches and attack passing birds. On nettles (Urtica). Migratory. Irruptive in some years. Common year-round in LA.

fm.Upperside: Black with red-orange forewing band; red hindwing border; white apical spots
fm.Underside: Cryptic dark mottling

hp: Larvae on Urtica (nettles). Adults feed on rotting fruit, tree sap, and flower nectar. Territorial — males defend sunny perches.

desc: Large eyespots on all four wings are the most prominent field mark of any LA butterfly. Open habitats. Uses plantain and snapdragon as hosts.

fm.Upperside: Brown; 2 forewing + 2 hindwing eyespots; orange bars
fm.Underside: Seasonally variable — tan/rose to deep brown

hp: Larvae on Plantago, Antirrhinum (snapdragon), Verbena. Prominent eyespots startle predators. Multiple broods year-round in SoCal.

desc: Among the longest-lived butterflies at 10-11 months. Dark maroon with pale yellow border. Overwinters as adult and flies on warm winter days.

fm.Upperside: Deep maroon with cream border and blue spot row
fm.Underside: Dark bark-like camouflage

hp: Mourning cloak — among longest-lived butterflies (10-11 months). Larvae on deciduous trees. Adults feed on sap and rotting fruit, not nectar.

desc: Medium orange-brown butterfly with dark borders. Famous for massive irruptive migrations in some years. Larvae on ceanothus.
hp: Larvae on Ceanothus (California lilac). Native chaparral specialist. Very large.

desc: Stunning riparian butterfly with bold white median band and orange forewing tips. Graceful gliding flight along streams through willow corridors.

fm.Upperside: Black with white median band; orange forewing tips
fm.Underside: Orange-brown with white band; blue-gray margins

hp: Part of local invertebrate food web. Associated with Red Willow.

desc: LA's signature oak woodland butterfly. Bold white band and orange forewing patches over dark chocolate background. Graceful gliding flight through live oak canopy.

fm.Upperside: Dark with bold white band; large orange apex patch
fm.Underside: Complex blue, orange, cream, brown bands

hp: Fagaceae: Quercus chrysolepis (canyon live oak)

desc: Small intricately patterned crescent butterfly. Low rapid flight through thistle patches. Multiple broods. Common in weedy habitats.

fm.Upperside: Orange with complex black reticulation
fm.Underside: Cream with tan patch and fine markings

hp: On thistles (Cirsium/Carduus) — Asteraceae.

desc: Large brown satyr with prominent yellow-ringed eyespots. Slow bobbing flight through grassy hillsides and meadow edges. Single summer brood.

fm.Upperside: Brown with 2 forewing eyespots in yellow patch
fm.Underside: Brown with eyespots and striping

hp: Acoustic signaler — song/stridulation important in mate location. Prey for bats and birds. Nymphalidae.

desc: The most common lawn skipper in urban LA. Rapid darting flight low over irrigated turf. Males bright orange; females darker. Year-round.

fm.Upperside: Bright orange (♂) with jagged border; brown (♀)
fm.Underside: Orange with scattered dark spots (♂)

hp: Poaceae: Bermuda grass, crabgrass, lawn grasses

desc: Dark oak-associated skipper. Key field mark: white hindwing fringe (diagnostic vs. other duskywings). Common in oak woodland year-round.

fm.Upperside: Dark brown-black; conspicuous white hindwing fringe
fm.Underside: Dark with bold white fringe

hp: Fagaceae: Quercus agrifolia (coast live oak), interior live oak

desc: Dark skipper with narrower white hindwing fringe than the similar Mournful Duskywing. Legume-feeding. Common year-round in warm areas.

fm.Upperside: Dark; narrow white hindwing fringe
fm.Underside: Dark with thin white fringe

hp: Fabaceae: Acmispon glaber (deerweed), Olneya, other legumes

desc: Chunky dark skipper with warm umber-brown tones. On irrigated lawns and gardens. One of the few skippers comfortable in heavily urbanized areas.

fm.Upperside: Dark brown with amber-orange spots
fm.Underside: Red-brown with paler spots

hp: Umber skipper — on Poaceae (grasses) in suburban gardens and grassland. Common small brown skipper. Larvae spin grass-blade shelters.

desc: Small boldly checkered black-and-white skipper. Males blue-gray when fresh. Extremely common in disturbed habitats and gardens. Fast darting flight.

fm.Upperside: Black with white checkering; ♂ blue-gray hairs
fm.Underside: White with olive-tan bands

hp: Malva, sida, sidalcea. In gardens.

desc: Small tawny skipper with prominent veined hindwing underside pattern in LA populations. Grassy areas. Common in irrigated habitats.

fm.Upperside: Tawny (♂) with dark borders
fm.Underside: Yellow-green to olive with pale veining

hp: LA County butterflies — habitat-specific ecological role. Hesperiidae.

desc: Plain gray-brown skipper, often overlooked. Very common in irrigated landscapes. One of the most abundant but least noticed LA butterflies.

fm.Upperside: Gray-brown; faint pale forewing spots
fm.Underside: Gray-brown with small pale hindwing spots

hp: Poaceae: various grasses inc. Bermuda, Johnson grass

desc: Bright white skipper with checkered fringes. Fast erratic flight. Arid hillsides and washes. Larvae on mallows. Desert-urban interface.

fm.Color: Bright white; checkered fringes; arid habitat

hp: Larvae on Malvaceae (mallow family) — Sphaeralcea, Malva, Sidalcea. Associated with Ash.

desc: LA's only metalmark butterfly. Intricate pattern of dark brown with white spots and rows of metallic silver-blue scales. Tightly associated with Eriogonum (buckwheat). Multiple localized subspecies. Small, with weak fluttery flight close to ground.

fm.Upperside: Dark brown checkered with orange/white; metallic silver scales
fm.Underside: Orange with black-white checkering and silver spots

hp: Mormon metalmark — LA's only metalmark. On Eriogonum fasciculatum (California buckwheat). Underside checkered in orange, white, and black.

desc: Dark reddish-brown elfin of coniferous forests. Hindwing underside rich mahogany with pale postmedian line. Associated with incense cedar and junipers. San Gabriel Mountains above 5,000 ft. Spring flier. Similar to Thicket but different host.

fm.Upperside: Dark reddish-brown
fm.Underside: Reddish-brown; irregular white postmedian line

hp: Part of local invertebrate food web. Forest habitat.

desc: Leda hairstreak — tiny blue-gray hairstreak with distinctive red-orange hindwing patches. Associated with mesquite. At desert-urban interface.

fm.Upperside: Blue-gray with orange marginal spots
fm.Underside: Gray with red-orange patches; white postmedian line

hp: Prosopis (mesquite). Desert habitat.

desc: Declining lupine-specialist blue butterfly. Once widespread, now restricted to montane sites where native lupines persist. Conservation concern.

fm.Upperside: Violet-blue (♂); dark brown with blue basal (♀)
fm.Underside: Gray with black spots in white halos; no orange band

hp: Boisduval's blue — on native Lupinus only. Declining with lupine habitat loss. Multiple subspecies, some federally listed elsewhere.

desc: Grayish lycaenid of riparian willows. Orange lunules on hindwing underside. Larvae on willows. Localized along riparian corridors.

fm.Color: Gray with orange HW lunules; on willows

hp: Sylvan hairstreak — on Salix (willows) in riparian corridors. Brown lycaenid. Declining with riparian habitat loss in SoCal.

desc: Distinguished from Painted Lady by two large (not four-five small) hindwing eyespots on the underside, plus a cobwebby white pattern. Forewing has a small white dot in the orange field. Caterpillars on Pseudognaphalium (cudweed). Year-round in LA.

fm.Upperside: Orange; small white spot in forewing cell
fm.Underside: Cobwebby pink-brown; 2 large hindwing eyespots
fm.Flight Period: Year-round; less common than V. cardui

hp: Asteraceae: Pseudognaphalium, Gnaphalium (cudweeds)

desc: Smaller and paler than Common Wood-Nymph. Dry grassy hillsides and chaparral edges. Slow bobbing flight. One or two eyespots on forewing.

fm.Upperside: Gray-brown; 1–2 small eyespots; minimal yellow
fm.Underside: Gray-brown with striations

hp: Native bunchgrasses.

desc: Historical record only — formerly bred on yucca in Antelope Valley. Not documented in LA County since early 20th century. Extirpated from most of SoCal range.

fm.Upperside: Dark brown-black with yellow forewing spots
fm.Underside: Dark with frosted gray overscaling; yellow spots

hp: Giant-skipper whose larvae bore inside Yucca stems/roots. Historical LA County record only.

desc: FEDERALLY ENDANGERED butterfly. Orange-and-black checkerspot. Severely declining due to habitat loss. Once common in coastal sage scrub.
hp: FEDERALLY ENDANGERED (63 FR 64370, 1998). Larvae on Plantago erecta. Habitat loss from development. Recovery plan active.

desc: Upland skipper with prominent silver-white chevron on hindwing underside. Mountain meadows and chaparral clearings. Late summer flier.

fm.Upperside: Orange (♂) with dark stigma; brown (♀)
fm.Underside: Olive/golden with silver-white chevron spots

hp: Leaf miner on host plant. Chaparral habitat. In chaparral habitat.

desc: Lindsey's skipper — coastal chaparral branded skipper. Flies earlier in season than the similar Western Branded Skipper. Hindwing underside olive-green with pale chevron spots. Restricted range — a California near-endemic. On native bunchgrasses.

fm.Upperside: Tawny orange (♂); brown (♀)
fm.Underside: Olive-green with pale elongated spots

hp: Olive (Olea europaea) pest — larvae in olive fruit. On olive.

desc: Desert subspecies of black swallowtail. Black with yellow spots. Larvae on desert parsley. At desert-urban interface.
hp: Larvae on specific host plants (Apiaceae, Aristolochiaceae, Rutaceae). Adults nectar on flowers.

desc: Larger than Sara Orangetip with bold green marbling on hindwing underside. In LA County restricted to San Gabriel Mountain peaks. Spring ephemeral.

fm.Upperside: White; bold black discal bar
fm.Underside: Bold green marbling

hp: Brassicaceae: mustards, Arabis

desc: Tiny blue butterfly with thin thread-like hindwing tails. At edge of western range in LA. Larvae on clovers and legumes. Very small.
hp: Larvae on Fabaceae — clover, alfalfa, and native legumes.

desc: Neotropical blue with single prominent round hindwing eyespot. Subtropical vagrant to LA. On legumes in weedy areas. Very small.

fm.Upperside: Blue (♂); brown (♀)
fm.Underside: Gray; single large hindwing eyespot

hp: Often ant-associated (myrmecophilous). Larvae on specific host plants. Some tend by ants. On Fabaceae (legumes).

desc: Small blue with prominent round spots in white halos on hindwing underside. Associated with mesquite and legumes. Desert and urban fringe.

fm.Upperside: Blue (♂); brown (♀)
fm.Underside: Row of round black spots in white halos

hp: On mesquite (prosopis) in desert habitat. Desert specialist.

desc: The most common anglewing in LA County. Golden orange with ragged wing edges and a metallic silver comma mark on hindwing underside. On nettles.

fm.Upperside: Golden-orange; dark spots; ragged edges
fm.Underside: Brown bark-like; silver comma

hp: Urticaceae: Urtica dioica (stinging nettle)

desc: Zephyr anglewing — mountain anglewing of high elevations. San Gabriel Mountains above 6,000 feet. Ragged wing edges and silver 'comma' on hindwing underside.

fm.Upperside: Orange-brown; dark spots; ragged edges
fm.Underside: Dark; silver comma mark

hp: Larvae on Ribes (gooseberry, currant). Mountain anglewing — above 6,000 ft in San Gabriels.

desc: Neotropical crescent butterfly that appears as an irregular vagrant to LA. Dark with cream-white band. Normally found in Texas and Mexico.

fm.Upperside: Dark with white median band; red basal
fm.Underside: Brown with pale banding

hp: Larvae on Acanthaceae (Siphonoglossa, Justicia). Vagrant from tropical Mexico to LA.

desc: The last skipper still flying in autumn. Common in fall on garden flowers. Tawny orange. Marks the transition to winter.

fm.Upperside: Tawny-orange; dark borders
fm.Underside: Yellow-orange with pale spot-band

hp: Citrus associate — on orange, lemon, grapefruit (Rutaceae). In gardens and urban areas.

desc: Tiny unmarked brown skipper easily overlooked in irrigated turf. One of the plainest butterflies in LA County. Common but rarely noticed.

fm.Upperside: Plain brown; faint pale spots
fm.Underside: Tan-brown; unmarked

hp: Larvae mostly on grasses (Poaceae) and legumes. Adults visit flowers. Hesperiidae.

desc: Tawny skipper of foothill grasslands. Angular silver-white spots on greenish hindwing underside. Spring and fall broods.

fm.Upperside: Tawny-orange; dark stigma
fm.Underside: Olive-green with silver spots

hp: On grasses and forbs in grassland. Grassland habitat.

desc: Gray-brown oak-feeding duskywing. Distinguished from Mournful by lack of white hindwing fringe. Chaparral and oak woodland.

fm.Upperside: Dark gray-brown; pale forewing spots; gray fringe
fm.Underside: Dark

hp: On coast live oak (quercus agrifolia). On Quercus (oak). In chaparral habitat. Propertius.

desc: Dark brown skipper whose larvae feed on ceanothus. Chaparral and woodland habitats. Rapid darting flight. Important ceanothus pollinator.

fm.Color: Dark brown with subtle markings; on ceanothus

hp: Larvae on Ceanothus (California lilac). Native chaparral specialist. In chaparral habitat. Pacuvius.

desc: Paler, more washed-out version of Common Checkered-Skipper. Desert habitats. More white, less bold pattern than its widespread relative.

fm.Upperside: Pale gray-white; sparse checkering
fm.Underside: Nearly white; olive bands

hp: Larvae mostly on grasses (Poaceae) and legumes. Adults visit flowers. Associated with Ash.

desc: Tawny orange skipper of woodland clearings and meadow edges. Larvae on grasses. Common in foothill oak woodland glades.
hp: Larvae on Quercus agrifolia (coast live oak). Oak woodland habitat. On oaks (Quercus).

desc: Small dark brown hairstreak of chaparral. Single spring brood. Perches with wings closed showing uniform brown underside.

fm.Upperside: Dark brown (rarely seen)
fm.Underside: Uniform warm brown; darker basal area

hp: Often ant-associated (myrmecophilous). Larvae on specific host plants. Some tend by ants. In chaparral habitat.

desc: Warm brown oak-feeding hairstreak named for Gold Rush era collectors who first found it. Orange submarginal spots and blue hindwing tail-spot below. On oaks. Montane. Males hilltop on ridges. Single summer brood.

fm.Upperside: Brown with faint orange spots
fm.Underside: Gray-brown; orange submarginal crescents; dark postmedian spots

hp: On coast live oak (quercus agrifolia). On Quercus (oak). Montane habitat.

desc: Cryptic gray-brown hairstreak closely associated with mountain mahogany (Cercocarpus). Extremely thin tails. Montane chaparral 4,000-8,000 ft. Distinguished from California Hairstreak by grayer tone and habitat/host plant fidelity.

fm.Upperside: Gray-brown
fm.Underside: Pale gray with dark postmedian line; small orange cap near tail

hp: Often ant-associated (myrmecophilous). Larvae on specific host plants. Some tend by ants. In chaparral habitat.

desc: Reddish-brown hairstreak with blue hindwing tails. Associated with dwarf mistletoe on conifers. Similar to Nelson's but warmer brown tones and different host association. San Gabriel Mountains at mid-elevations. Single spring brood.

fm.Upperside: Brown
fm.Underside: Reddish-brown with white postmedian line bordered blue

hp: Often ant-associated (myrmecophilous). Larvae on specific host plants. Some tend by ants. Lycaenidae.

desc: Bright green underside like Bramble Hairstreak but on junipers rather than buckwheat. Desert edge and montane chaparral.

fm.Upperside: Brown
fm.Underside: Bright green with white postmedian line
fm.Habitat: Juniper woodland, desert edge with junipers

hp: On california buckwheat (eriogonum). On Eriogonum (buckwheat). In chaparral habitat.

desc: Bright emerald green underside like Bramble Hairstreak but duller, smaller, on buckwheat rather than lotus. Single spring brood in chaparral. Fades to gray-brown after death — museum specimens lose the green.

fm.Upperside: Brown
fm.Underside: Gray-green; white postmedian line sometimes reduced
fm.Habitat: Coastal sage scrub, chaparral with buckwheat

hp: On california buckwheat (eriogonum). On Eriogonum (buckwheat). In chaparral habitat.

desc: Tiny blue tightly associated with Dudleya (liveforever succulents). One of earliest spring butterflies. Rocky coastal bluffs.

fm.Upperside: Blue (♂); brown with blue base (♀); orange HW spots
fm.Underside: White with black spots; orange HW band
fm.Habitat: Rocky coastal bluffs, cliff faces with Dudleya

hp: Often ant-associated (myrmecophilous). Larvae on specific host plants. Some tend by ants. Associated with Liveforever.

desc: White arrowhead-shaped spots on gray hindwing underside are diagnostic (unique among LA blues). Lupine specialist. Single spring brood. Mountain meadows and chaparral openings. Declining with habitat loss — some populations extirpated.

fm.Upperside: Blue-violet (♂); brown with blue base (♀)
fm.Underside: Gray with white arrowhead-shaped HW spots

hp: Arrowhead blue — larvae on Lupinus with ant associations. Myrmecophilous.

desc: Tiny pale blue with threadlike hindwing tails. Among the smallest LA butterflies after Pygmy Blue. Mesic habitats — stream edges, moist meadows. Larvae on legumes (Lathyrus, Vicia). Spring-summer.

fm.Upperside: Pale blue (♂); gray (♀)
fm.Underside: Pale gray-white with scattered dark dots

hp: Often ant-associated (myrmecophilous). Larvae on specific host plants. Some tend by ants. On Fabaceae (legumes).

desc: Orange-and-black checkerspot of mountain meadows and clearings. Distinguished from Mylitta by larger size, higher elevation habitat, and broader pale bands. Larvae on Aster and Castilleja. San Gabriel Mountains. Single summer brood.

fm.Upperside: Orange with complex black pattern and cream spots
fm.Underside: Cream and orange with dark-outlined cells

hp: Part of local invertebrate food web. Associated with Indian Paintbrush.

desc: Boldly patterned black checkerspot with cream bands and orange patches. Multiple SoCal subspecies. Rocky chaparral with paintbrush host plants.

fm.Upperside: Black with cream/white bands; orange patches
fm.Underside: Cream with orange and black pattern

hp: Native chaparral scrub habitat. In chaparral habitat. Leanira.

desc: Large orange fritillary at range edge in the San Gabriel Mountains. Greenish underside with silver spots. Mountain meadows.

fm.Upperside: Tawny; dark spots
fm.Underside: Greenish with burnished silver spots

hp: Larvae on Viola (violets). Mountain fritillary at range edge in San Gabriel Mountains.

desc: Arizona red-spotted purple — a Batesian mimic of Pipevine Swallowtail with iridescent blue-black wings and red-orange ventral spots. The western subspecies of the polymorphic White Admiral/Red-spotted Purple complex. Extremely rare vagrant to desert-edge LA.

fm.Upperside: Blue-black iridescence; no white band
fm.Underside: Dark with orange submarginal spots; blue marginal area
fm.Habitat: Riparian; rare vagrant to LA desert edge

hp: Larvae on Aristolochia californica (pipevine). Sequesters toxins for chemical defense. Desert specialist.

desc: Subtropical butterfly with pale gray-white wings and orange forewing patch. Normal range is Mexico and South Texas — reaches LA County very rarely as a vagrant. Any SoCal record is notable. On Tragia (noseburn). Small, weak flight.

fm.Upperside: White-gray with orange forewing costal patch
fm.Underside: White with faint banding
fm.Flight: Year-round in tropics; vagrant to LA

hp: Part of local invertebrate food web. Nymphalidae.

desc: Large robust dark skipper with pale forewing spots. Desert canyons with locust trees (Robinia). Normal range is Arizona and Mexico — extremely rare vagrant to LA County. Any sighting represents a major range extension.

fm.Upperside: Dark brown with faint pale spots
fm.Underside: Dark with violet sheen

hp: Larvae mostly on grasses (Poaceae) and legumes. Adults visit flowers. Desert specialist.

desc: Liban skipper — small dark checkered skipper restricted to desert saltbush flats. Specialist on Atriplex (saltbush). One of the most habitat-restricted LA skippers.

fm.Upperside: Black-brown with small white checkers
fm.Underside: Dark with scattered white spots

hp: Larvae mostly on grasses (Poaceae) and legumes. Adults visit flowers. Desert specialist.

desc: Small dark skipper with white head spots. Associated with Amaranthaceae (pigweed, lamb's-quarters). Weedy urban lots. Easily overlooked.

fm.Upperside: Black-brown with fine white flecking; white head spots
fm.Underside: Black-brown with faint pale spots

hp: Larvae mostly on grasses (Poaceae) and legumes. Adults visit flowers. Urban habitat.

desc: Large orange skipper. Males with large brown stigma on forewing. Females distinctively patterned with glassy forewing spots. Common on lawns and gardens.

fm.Upperside: ♂: bright orange with dark borders. ♀: dark with orange spots.
fm.Underside: ♂: orange-tan with large diffuse brown HW patch (diagnostic)

hp: Poaceae: Bermuda grass, crabgrass, Dallis grass

desc: Subspecies of Western Branded Skipper. Late-summer flier in SoCal mountains; grasses. Distinguished from other Hesperia by ventral hindwing pattern.

fm.Upperside: Orange-brown; dark margins
fm.Underside: Rust-orange with cream chevron spots

hp: On grasses and forbs in grassland. On Poaceae (grasses). Hesperiidae.

desc: Large robust skipper with prominent silver-white patch on hindwing underside (unique — no other NA skipper has this). Strong rapid flight. On legumes. Widespread east of the Rockies but irregular in LA — may be colonizing.

fm.Upperside: Dark brown with gold FW spots; elongated HW
fm.Underside: Dark with large silver-white HW patch (diagnostic)
fm.Habitat: Woodland edges, gardens with legume vines

hp: Larvae mostly on grasses (Poaceae) and legumes. Adults visit flowers. On Fabaceae (legumes).

desc: Neotropical skipper with brilliant iridescent blue-green body and remarkably long hindwing tails. Larvae on legumes. Normal range is tropical/subtropical — rare vagrant to SoCal in warm years. Unmistakable when seen.

fm.Upperside: Dark with iridescent blue-green thorax; long HW tails
fm.Underside: Brown with dark bands; long tails
fm.Flight: Year-round in tropics; vagrant to LA summer–fall

hp: Larvae mostly on grasses (Poaceae) and legumes. Adults visit flowers. On Fabaceae (legumes).

desc: Small buckwheat specialist blue. Distinguished from Square-spotted Blue by round (not squarish) metallic marginal spots on hindwing underside. Multiple local subspecies with subtle differences. On various Eriogonum species. Single brooded, timed to buckwheat bloom.

fm.Upperside: Blue (♂); brown (♀)
fm.Underside: Gray; orange HW band with round (not square) spots
fm.Flight: Apr–Jul; single brood sync with Eriogonum
fm.Habitat: Coastal sage, chaparral slopes with buckwheat

hp: Specialist on Eriogonum (buckwheat) flowers. Critical plant-pollinator relationship.

desc: Reddish-brown elfin of chaparral slopes. Jagged postmedian line on underside distinctive. On manzanita and ceanothus. Early spring flier.

fm.Upperside: Dark brown
fm.Underside: Rich reddish-brown with irregular/jagged pale postmedian line

hp: Larvae on Ceanothus (California lilac). Native chaparral specialist. In chaparral habitat. Western.

desc: A blue of mountain meadows. Similar to Acmon but typically at higher elevation. Orange HW band usually narrower.

fm.Upperside: Blue (♂); brown with orange band (♀)
fm.Underside: Gray; orange HW band typically narrower than Acmon

hp: Often ant-associated (myrmecophilous). Larvae on specific host plants. Some tend by ants. Lycaenidae.

desc: Very large yellow-and-black swallowtail. Largest butterfly in LA County (5+ in wingspan). Larvae on citrus ('orange dogs'). Expanding range northward.
hp: Rutaceae — Citrus, Zanthoxylum, Ptelea, Ruta. Important prey for insectivorous birds.

desc: A large, showy checkerspot common in chaparral and coastal sage scrub. Highly variable — populations range from predominantly black to heavily orange/red. Larvae feed communally in silk webs on host plants. 1,863 LA County iNat obs

fm.Upperside: Black with red/orange/cream checkered spots; highly variable between populations
fm.Underside: Cream/yellow with red and black bands and spots
fm.Host Plants: Scrophulariaceae — Diplacus (monkeyflower), Castilleja, Penstemon; also Plantago
fm.Habitat: Chaparral, coastal sage scrub, oak woodland edges

hp: Scrophulariaceae — Diplacus (monkeyflower), Castilleja, Penstemon; also Plantago

desc: Recently split from Common Buckeye (J. coenia). The western/dry-habitat species — grayer overall, eyespots tend smaller. The dominant buckeye in arid LA County habitats.

fm.Upperside: Gray-brown (less warm than J. coenia) with eyespots; eyespots may average smaller
fm.Underside: Variable — tan to rose-brown; often grayer than coenia
fm.Habitat: Drier, more arid habitats than J. coenia — desert edges, dry washes, chaparral trails

hp: Plantaginaceae: Plantago; Orobanchaceae: Mimulus, snapdragons

desc: Most common small blue in LA chaparral. Orange HW marginal band with metallic green caps diagnostic. Genus recently moved from Plebejus to Icaricia on iNat. 1,482 LA County obs

fm.Upperside: Blue (♂) or brown with orange marginal band (♀)
fm.Underside: Gray with black spots; vivid orange HW band with metallic green caps
fm.Habitat: Chaparral, coastal sage scrub, rocky hillsides, trails

hp: Acmon blue — most common small blue in LA chaparral. Larvae on Eriogonum, Lotus, Astragalus. Myrmecophilous — ant-tended larvae.

desc: LA County's second metalmark (alongside Mormon). Smaller, darker, more common in warm desert-edge habitats. Tight buckwheat association.

fm.Upperside: Dark brown-black with white spots in regular rows; metallic scales
fm.Underside: Gray-brown with white spots and metallic silver scaling
fm.Habitat: Coastal sage scrub, desert scrub, chaparral — warm rocky slopes with buckwheat

hp: Eriogonum fasciculatum (California buckwheat)

desc: Paler and more washed-out than the Common Checkered-Skipper. Desert and arid habitats. Distinguished by reduced dark markings and more extensive white areas.

fm.Upperside: Pale gray-white with sparse dark checkering; ♂ blue-gray hairs fresh
fm.Underside: Nearly white with faint olive-tan bands
fm.Habitat: Desert washes, arid scrub, open disturbed ground

hp: Malvaceae — Sphaeralcea, Malva

desc: A buckwheat-specialist blue. Distinguished from Square-spotted Blue by range and subtle underside pattern differences.

fm.Upperside: Blue (♂); brown (♀)
fm.Underside: Gray with black spots; orange HW marginal band
fm.Flight Period: May–Sep; single brood, sync with buckwheat bloom
fm.Habitat: Coastal sage scrub, chaparral slopes with buckwheat

hp: Bernardino blue — oligolectic on Eriogonum fasciculatum flowers only. Larvae eat flowers, not leaves. Buckwheat specialist.

desc: LA County's third metalmark — associated with Baccharis in riparian and desert wash habitats. "Fatal" name from original description, not toxicity.

fm.Upperside: Dark brown with darker median bands; metallic silver-gray spots
fm.Underside: Orange-brown with metallic silver spots
fm.Flight Period: Year-round in warm areas; multiple broods
fm.Habitat: Desert washes, riparian edges with Baccharis

hp: Baccharis sarothroides (desert broom), B. Salicifolia

desc: Federally Endangered. Restricted to remnant coastal dune habitat at LAX and nearby sites. One of the rarest butterflies in North America. iNat name differs from our previous entry — updating. (mostly from managed habitat).

fm.Upperside: Blue (♂); brown (♀)
fm.Underside: Gray with prominent black spots; orange marginal band
fm.Flight Period: Jun–Aug; single brood, sync with coast buckwheat
fm.Habitat: Coastal dune scrub — El Segundo dunes, LAX area

hp: Eriogonum parvifolium (coast buckwheat) — obligate

desc: Federally Endangered. Once thought extinct, rediscovered 1994. Restricted to coastal sage scrub on Palos Verdes Peninsula — one of the rarest butterflies in the world. Captive breeding program ongoing. LA County endemic.

fm.Upperside: ♂: silvery-blue with narrow black border. ♀: brownish-gray.
fm.Underside: Gray with dark spots ringed in white
fm.Host Plants: Astragalus trichopodus var. lonchus, Acmispon glaber (deerweed)
fm.Habitat: Coastal sage scrub — Palos Verdes Peninsula only

hp: Astragalus trichopodus var. Lonchus, Acmispon glaber (deerweed)

desc: Orange-and-black checkerspot of chaparral. Named for William Gabb, California geological surveyor. Closely tied to Hazardia squarrosa host plant.

fm.Upperside: Orange with black markings; cream spots near margins
fm.Underside: Cream/yellow with orange and black patterning
fm.Habitat: Coastal sage scrub, chaparral, rocky hillsides

hp: Hazardia squarrosa (saw-toothed goldenbush)

desc: California ringlet — small bouncy satyr of grasslands and meadows. Pale tawny with simple eyespot below. Low bobbing flight. The California population, recently recognized as distinct from C. tullia.

fm.Upperside: Plain tawny-orange
fm.Underside: Olive-tan with small silver-pupiled eyespot; gray hindwing

hp: LA County butterflies — habitat-specific ecological role. Grassland habitat.

desc: A coastal salt marsh specialist — one of the most habitat-restricted skippers in California. Brown with faint pale spots. Strong flier that wanders from marsh habitat.

fm.Upperside: Dark brown with faint pale forewing spots
fm.Underside: Brown with faint purplish sheen; pale vein streaks
fm.Habitat: Coastal salt marshes — Ballona Wetlands, Malibu Lagoon, other remnant marshes

hp: Distichlis spicata (saltgrass) — obligate

desc: A Neotropical sulphur reaching LA County. Larger and more orange than Cloudless Sulphur. Irregular vagrant/colonizer.

fm.Upperside: ♂: deep orange with subtle darker margins. ♀: paler, yellow-orange.
fm.Underside: Yellow-orange with small reddish cell spots
fm.Habitat: Gardens, open areas during immigration years

hp: Fabaceae: Pithecellobium, Senna (non-native)

desc: Distinctive copper-orange butterfly with delicate hindwing tail. Associated with wild currant (Ribes) in riparian and canyon habitats. Males territorial.

fm.Upperside: ♂: bright copper-orange. ♀: brown with orange highlights.
fm.Underside: Gray-white with black spots; thin hindwing tail
fm.Habitat: Mountain chaparral, oak woodland with Ribes understory

hp: Part of local invertebrate food web. Associated with Golden Currant.

desc: Catalina Island endemic — one of the most geographically restricted butterflies in North America. Gray with blue hindwing scaling. Deerweed specialist. (mostly Catalina).

fm.Upperside: Gray with blue-purple tint, especially ♂
fm.Underside: Pale gray; dark postmedian line; small orange/black HW spots near tail
fm.Habitat: Coastal sage scrub on Santa Catalina Island (LA County)

hp: Acmispon glaber (deerweed) — near-obligate

desc: Bright orange pierid of warm lowlands. Named for the closed-eye appearance of its wing undersides when perched. Seasonally variable — dry-form with heavier markings.

fm.Upperside: Bright orange with irregular black borders
fm.Underside: Seasonally variable — yellow (summer) to brick-red (winter)
fm.Habitat: Desert washes, warm open areas, urban edges

hp: Citrus associate — on orange, lemon, grapefruit (Rutaceae). Pieridae.

desc: A large, boldly marked white of desert-edge habitats. Green marbling on HW underside heavier than Checkered White. Desert-adapted.

fm.Upperside: White with heavy dark markings especially in ♀; greenish tint fresh
fm.Underside: HW heavily marbled with yellow-green (bolder than Checkered White)
fm.Habitat: Desert washes, alkaline flats, desert-edge with Brassicaceae

hp: Brassicaceae — Stanleya pinnata (prince's plume), Descurainia

desc: Large copper with intricate spotted underside on buckwheat. Males with purple-gray upperside. Chaparral hillsides. Spring flier.

fm.Upperside: ♂: bright copper with dark spots. ♀: brown-orange with heavy spotting.
fm.Underside: Gray-tan with black spots; orange HW marginal band
fm.Habitat: Coastal sage scrub, chaparral with buckwheat

hp: Eriogonum fasciculatum.

desc: A mountain crescent similar to Mylitta but typically at higher elevations with Aster host plants (not thistles).

fm.Upperside: Orange with black reticulation; broader dark markings than Mylitta
fm.Underside: Cream/yellow with brown patch; pattern more contrasting than Mylitta

hp: Asteraceae — Aster, Erigeron (not Cirsium)

desc: Verity's blue — high San Gabriel Mountains endemic, one of LA County's rarest resident butterflies. Associated with Astragalus and Lotus at elevation. Extremely localized.

fm.Upperside: Blue (♂); brown (♀)
fm.Underside: Gray with dark spots; veined appearance
fm.Habitat: High San Gabriel Mtns above 7,000' — extremely restricted

hp: Part of local invertebrate food web. Associated with Coulter Pine.

desc: The largest NA copper — almost hairstreak-sized. Distinctive pale underside with large black spots. Dock specialist.

fm.Upperside: ♂: gray with orange submarginal spots. ♀: orange with dark spots.
fm.Underside: White/cream with bold round black spots; orange submarginal HW band
fm.Habitat: Moist meadows, streambanks, agricultural edges with dock

hp: Rumex (docks) — especially R. Hymenosepalus

desc: Desert checkerspot with orange, black, and cream pattern. Larvae on sagebrush (Artemisia). Desert margins and Mojave-facing slopes of San Gabriel Mountains. Distinguished from Leanira by habitat (sagebrush vs. paintbrush) and paler cream spots.

fm.Upperside: Orange with black markings; cream marginal spots
fm.Underside: Cream with orange bands and black outlines

hp: Larvae on Castilleja (paintbrush). Checkerspot — host plant specialist.

desc: Higher-elevation blue with broader orange aurora band than Acmon Blue. Lupine specialist. Mountain meadows and forest openings.

fm.Upperside: Blue (♂); brown with broader orange band (♀)
fm.Underside: Like Acmon but orange band broader

hp: Lupinus, astragalus. Mountain habitat.

desc: Tawny mountain skipper with prominent pale chevron spots on olive-green hindwing underside. Grassland specialist of montane meadows in San Gabriel Mountains. Strong fast flight. Single summer brood. Distinguished from Juba by more angular spot shapes.

fm.Upperside: Tawny-orange (♂); brown (♀)
fm.Underside: Olive-green with pale chevron spot-band

hp: Leaf miner on host plant. Montane habitat.

desc: Vivid orange forewing tips like Sara Orangetip but hindwing underside with more yellow tone and finer green marbling. A desert spring ephemeral — adults fly for only a few weeks. On Brassicaceae. Desert canyons and washes.

fm.Upperside: White/yellow with orange FW tips (♂); reduced (♀)
fm.Underside: HW marbled green on yellow ground (more yellow than Sara)
fm.Habitat: Desert washes, desert scrub, Joshua tree woodland

hp: Brassicaceae — desert mustards, Caulanthus

desc: Large orange fritillary with silver spots on hindwing underside. Montane meadows in San Gabriel Mountains. At range edge. Spring-summer flier.

fm.Upperside: Tawny orange; black spots and bars
fm.Underside: Olive-brown with smaller silver spots

hp: Viola pedunculata (Johnny-jump-up)

desc: Unusual hairstreak — golden-brown rather than gray. Associated with canyon live oak at mid-elevations. Single-brooded.

fm.Upperside: Golden-brown; smooth unmarked
fm.Underside: Golden-tan with faint darker postmedian line; no tails
fm.Habitat: Canyon live oak woodland, rocky canyon slopes

hp: Quercus chrysolepis (canyon live oak) — near-obligate

desc: Desert buckwheat specialist. Distinguished from Bernardino by range (Mojave side) and minor pattern differences.

fm.Upperside: Blue (♂); brown (♀)
fm.Underside: Gray; orange HW band; spots slightly different from bernardino

hp: Mojave blue — desert Eriogonum specialist. Larvae feed exclusively on buckwheat flowers. Mojave Desert margin populations.

desc: Large orange fritillary with elongated silver spots on greenish hindwing underside. Mountain meadows in San Gabriel Mountains. Strong swift flight.

fm.Upperside: Orange with dark spots; black veins
fm.Underside: HW with prominent silver spots on greenish-brown ground

hp: Viola (violets). Mountain habitat.

desc: Unmistakable — elongated palps form a "snout." Orange-and-brown pattern. Hackberry specialist. Rare in LA County — mostly desert edge.

fm.Upperside: Brown with orange patches; elongated snout-like palps (diagnostic)
fm.Underside: Gray-brown; leaf-like camouflage when closed
fm.Flight Period: Year-round in tropics; vagrant to LA

hp: Cannabaceae: Celtis reticulata (netleaf hackberry)

desc: Neotropical. Long narrow wings with bold black and yellow stripes. Communal rooster. Occasional vagrant to SoCal — may establish in warm years with Passiflora.

fm.Upperside: Black with bold yellow stripes
fm.Underside: Similar with red basal spots
fm.Flight Period: Year-round in tropics; vagrant to LA summer–fall
fm.Habitat: Gardens with passionvine during vagrant events

hp: Larvae on Passiflora (passionflower) vines. Neotropical vagrant to LA. Pollen-feeding adult — unique.

desc: Small gray-green hairstreak of desert-edge habitats with antelope bitterbrush (Purshia). Rare at western range edge in LA County. Thinly tailed. Orange submarginal spots on underside. Named for German-born California entomologist H.H. Behr.

fm.Upperside: Gray-brown
fm.Underside: Gray-green with thin white postmedian line; small orange spot near tail

hp: Part of local invertebrate food web. Desert habitat. Desert specialist. Behr's.

desc: Montane branded skipper with jagged and angular greenish spots on hindwing underside. Mountain meadows and chaparral clearings. Strong fast flight.

fm.Upperside: Tawny-orange (♂); brown (♀)
fm.Underside: Olive-green with large jagged/angular white spots

hp: LA County butterflies — habitat-specific ecological role. In chaparral habitat.

desc: Medium yellow butterfly of foothill chaparral. California endemic. On deer weed and other native legumes. Uncommon — declining.
hp: Fabaceae: Astragalus spp., Acmispon spp.

desc: Desert spring white with bold green marbling on hindwing underside. Very early spring flier in desert canyons — one of the first butterflies each year. On Brassicaceae. Similar to Large Marble but in desert habitats and earlier season.

fm.Upperside: White; black discal spot on FW
fm.Underside: HW with bold irregular green marble pattern on white

hp: Brassicaceae — desert mustards

desc: Extremely pale buckwheat specialist. One of the most localized butterflies in LA County. Desert buckwheat flats. Distinguished by very faded wing pattern.

fm.Upperside: Pale blue (♂); pale brown (♀)
fm.Underside: Very pale gray; reduced spotting

hp: Eriogonum — specific desert species

desc: Dark, robust spread-wing skipper. White forewing spots small, aligned in an arc. Face with white patch. Distinguished from Funereal Duskywing by broader build and white face. Ceanothus feeder. Very rare in LA County — primarily montane.

fm.Upperside: Dark brown with aligned white FW spots in an arc
fm.Underside: Dark brown with faint pale bands

hp: Larvae on Ceanothus (California lilac). Native chaparral specialist. Montane habitat.

desc: Old World citrus pest swallowtail — NOT native. Three LA records may represent accidental importation on citrus shipments or deliberate releases. Invasive threat if established.

fm.Upperside: Black/brown with scattered yellow spots (not banded like native swallowtails)
fm.Underside: Yellow with blue and red HW spots
fm.Host Plants: Rutaceae — Citrus (major pest in Old World)

hp: Rutaceae — Citrus (major pest in Old World). Important prey for insectivorous birds.

desc: Yellow sulphur with dark forewing 'dog face' pattern. Similar to California Dogface but smaller and less vivid. Desert-edge. On indigo bush.

fm.Upperside: ♂: yellow with dark "dogface" outline. ♀: yellow-green.
fm.Underside: Greenish-yellow; pink cell spot

hp: Dalea, amorpha, trifolium. Desert habitat.

desc: Tiny pale skipper restricted to alkaline desert flats. One of the most habitat-specialized butterflies. Saltgrass dependent. Extremely localized.

fm.Upperside: Pale orange-yellow; narrow dark borders
fm.Underside: Pale yellow; unmarked or faint veining

hp: Distichlis spicata (saltgrass)

desc: Bold orange, cream, and black desert checkerspot. Larvae on Ambrosia and Viguiera. A Sonoran Desert species that reaches LA County as an extreme vagrant from Colorado Desert. Any sighting is noteworthy.

fm.Upperside: Bold orange and cream patches on black ground
fm.Underside: Cream with orange and black pattern
fm.Habitat: Sonoran desert scrub; vagrant to LA basin

hp: Part of local invertebrate food web. Associated with Western Ragweed.

desc: A tailless Neotropical swallowtail with gold-edged black wings. Normal range is tropical Americas — extremely rare vagrant to SoCal. Larvae feed exclusively on pipevines (Aristolochia). Any LA County record represents a major rarity event.

fm.Upperside: Black with gold/yellow submarginal band; no tails
fm.Underside: Black with red basal spots; gold band

hp: Larvae on Aristolochia californica (pipevine). Sequesters toxins for chemical defense. Ant associate.

desc: Small reddish-brown elfin of rocky habitats with stonecrop (Dudleya). Very localized — found only where host plant grows on rocky outcrops.

fm.Upperside: Brown
fm.Underside: Reddish-brown basally; frosted gray outwardly; jagged line between

hp: Larvae on Dudleya (liveforever succulents). Specialist on cliff and rock face plants.

desc: Subtropical hairstreak reaching its northern range limit in LA. Associated with mallows. Rare — among the most infrequently recorded hairstreaks.

fm.Upperside: Gray-brown
fm.Underside: Pale gray; dark postmedian line; small spots

hp: Larvae on Malvaceae (mallow family) — Sphaeralcea, Malva, Sidalcea. Associated with Mallows.

desc: Tiny tropical hairstreak with brilliant metallic green underside crossed by a silver band. Subtropical vagrant. On balloon vine.

fm.Upperside: Dark with purple sheen (♂)
fm.Underside: Brilliant green; silver-white postmedian band

hp: Fabaceae — Pithecellobium, balloon vine

desc: Large dark skipper whose larvae bore inside living yucca stalks, creating silk-lined tunnels. Adults rapid and powerful. Desert margins.

fm.Upperside: Dark brown-black with yellow FW spots and narrow yellow HW border
fm.Underside: Dark with frosted gray overscaling
fm.Host Plants: Yucca spp. — larvae bore in living root crown
fm.Habitat: Desert yucca grasslands, Joshua tree woodland

hp: Wood/stem borer. Associated with Chaparral Yucca.

desc: Recently described and split from Woodland Skipper. Taxonomy still being actively resolved by lepidopterists. Very similar to Woodland — may require genitalic examination for confident ID. SoCal populations currently assigned here.

fm.Upperside: Tawny-orange
fm.Underside: Pale yellow-orange with spot-band

hp: Larvae on native bunchgrasses (Poaceae: Stipa, Nassella). Recently split from Woodland Skipper.

desc: Tropical nymphalid with pale white wings and orange margins. Single LA County obs— extreme vagrant. Establishing in south Florida/Texas.

fm.Upperside: White with small brown spots; orange HW margin and eyespot
fm.Underside: Similar but paler
fm.Habitat: Tropical wetlands; extreme vagrant to LA

hp: Acanthaceae — Ruellia, Blechum

desc: Famous Monarch mimic. Not a regular LA County resident — single obslikely a stray. Distinguished from Monarch by black postmedian HW line.

fm.Upperside: Orange with black veins (like Monarch) PLUS black postmedian HW line (diagnostic)
fm.Underside: Like Monarch but with black HW crossline
fm.Habitat: Riparian woodland; extreme vagrant to LA

hp: Viceroy — famous Monarch mimic. Not a regular LA County resident. Larvae on Salix and Populus elsewhere but vagrant to SoCal.

desc: Large bright orange neotropical sulphur. Only a single LA County observation — extreme vagrant from tropical Mexico. Spectacular if seen.

fm.Upperside: ♂: bright orange with orange bar. ♀: yellow with pink-orange patches.
fm.Underside: Yellow with pink/red cell spots

hp: Citrus associate — on orange, lemon, grapefruit (Rutaceae). Ant associate.

desc: Desert-edge alkali specialist found at scattered sites with saltgrass. Small pale blue. Named for Fort San Emigdio area. Very localized.

fm.Upperside: Blue (♂); brown (♀)
fm.Underside: White with black dots; no orange band

hp: San Emigdio blue — desert-edge Astragalus specialist. Scattered alkali flat populations with Distichlis (saltgrass). Range-restricted.

desc: One of the tiniest skippers, entirely unmarked orange above. Rapid low flight through grass. Desert margins and foothill meadows.

fm.Upperside: Bright unmarked orange
fm.Underside: Orange, unmarked

hp: Bermuda, cynodon. Desert habitat.

desc: Black with yellow spot rows. Blue HW wash (♀). Larvae on carrot family (parsley, dill, fennel). Occasional in LA — at edge of western range.

fm.Color: Black with yellow spots; ♀ blue HW wash

hp: Larvae on specific host plants (Apiaceae, Aristolochiaceae, Rutaceae). Adults nectar on flowers. Associated with Ash.

desc: Small dark skipper butterfly. Larvae on scrub oaks. Early spring flier in chaparral. Wings held flat at rest (unlike most butterflies).
hp: Larvae on Quercus agrifolia (coast live oak). Oak woodland habitat. In chaparral habitat.
